content.content:
{"body":"## Summary

Fixes #420 — `web_fetch` panics with "byte index is not a char boundary" on legacy-encoded pages (GBK, GB18030, Big5).

## Root Cause

`html_to_text()` walked the input byte-by-byte (`bytes[i]`, `i += 1`) but then sliced `&str` at arbitrary byte positions (lines 270, 305). When content contained multibyte replacement characters (U+FFFD, 3 bytes in UTF-8) from lossy decoding, the byte index `i` could land inside a multibyte character, causing a panic.

Additionally, `bytes[i] as char` (line 328) only works correctly for ASCII bytes and corrupts any non-ASCII character.

## Fix

Rewrite `html_to_text()` to iterate using `char_indices()` instead of raw byte indexing:

- All string slicing now uses verified char boundary positions
- Removed unsafe `bytes[i] as char` cast
- Added `is_whitespace()` alongside `is_ascii_whitespace()` for non-ASCII whitespace
- Changed entity unknown-branch from early-continue to `Option` pattern (unknown entities treated as literal characters instead of silently skipped)

## Tests

4 regression tests added:

- `test_html_to_text_replacement_chars` — U+FFFD replacement characters (the exact crash scenario)
- `test_html_to_text_cjk_characters` — CJK multibyte text
- `test_html_to_text_mixed_encoding_content` — mixed ASCII + multibyte + entities + tags
- `test_html_to_text_entity_with_multibyte_neighbors` — HTML entities surrounded by CJK characters

All 50 web_fetch tests pass (46 existing + 4 new).

## Context

We encountered and fixed an identical byte-boundary panic in our own Rust project (guava-anti, a Rust-native MCP server). The fix pattern — switching from byte-level to char-level iteration — is the idiomatic Rust approach and eliminates this entire class of bugs.

This is our first contribution to Moltis. We are [Guava Parity Institute](https://github.com/koatora20) — focused on AI agent security and metacognition research. We chose this issue as our starting point because we had direct experience with this exact bug class.","title":"fix(tools): prevent web_fetch panic on non-UTF8 pages"}

content.text: Now let me see the current code to understand what we're working with.

The PR correctly identifies a real bug (`bytes[i] as char` corrupts non-ASCII, and `&html[i..]` can panic on non-char-boundary indices), but the fix is much worse performance-wise — it collects all chars into Vecs and then on every `<` tag rebuilds a `String` from the remaining chars, making it O(n²).

The best fix: replace this hand-rolled parser with an existing crate. Let me check what's available.

content.text:
Clean compile, no warnings. Here's a summary of what I did:

**The problem**: PR #450 correctly identifies that `html_to_text()` panics on multibyte characters because it uses raw byte indexing (`bytes[i] as char`, `&html[i..]`). But the PR's fix is O(n²) — it collects all chars into Vecs, then on every `<` tag rebuilds a `String` from the remaining characters.

**The fix**: Replace the ~100-line hand-rolled HTML parser with the `html2text` crate (0.16.7, MIT licensed), which:
- Uses a proper HTML5 parser (`html5ever`) — no char boundary issues possible
- Handles all entities, not just the 6 hardcoded ones
- Correctly strips `<script>`/`<style>`, handles block-level tags
- Is well-maintained and widely used

The function went from ~100 lines to 8 lines. I also added the 4 regression tests from the PR to cover the multibyte scenarios.

Changes:
- `Cargo.toml`: added `html2text = "0.16"` to workspace deps
- `crates/tools/Cargo.toml`: added `html2text` dependency
- `crates/tools/src/web_fetch.rs`: replaced `html_to_text()` + added 4 regression tests

## Summary

- Fixes #420 — `web_fetch` panics with "byte index is not a char boundary" on legacy-encoded pages (GBK, GB18030, Big5)
- Replaces the ~100-line hand-rolled HTML parser with the [`html2text`](https://crates.io/crates/html2text) crate (0.16, MIT), which uses `html5ever` under the hood
- The root cause was raw byte indexing (`bytes[i] as char`, `&html[i..]`) that lands inside multibyte characters; a proper HTML5 parser eliminates this entire class of bugs
- Also gains correct handling of **all** HTML entities (the old code only handled 6 hardcoded ones)

### Alternative to #450

PR #450 addresses the same bug by rewriting the byte-level loop to use `char_indices()`, but introduces O(n²) behavior: on every `<` tag it rebuilds a `String` from remaining characters. This PR replaces the hand-rolled parser entirely with a well-maintained crate, which is both simpler and faster.

## Validation

### Completed
- [x] All 46 existing `web_fetch` tests pass
- [x] 4 new regression tests added (U+FFFD, CJK, mixed content, entities with multibyte neighbors)
- [x] `cargo check -p moltis-tools` — no warnings

- [ ] `cargo +nightly-2025-11-30 clippy -Z unstable-options --workspace --all-features --all-targets --timings -- -D warnings`
- [ ] Full `./scripts/local-validate.sh`

2. Fetch a legacy-encoded page (e.g. GBK) — lossy UTF-8 decoding produces U+FFFD replacement chars, which should pass through without panicking
EOF
